title: scenario_vishing
description: An attacker impersonates IT support over the phone to trick an employee into revealing their credentials and installing remote access software.

timeline.title: Call Received
timeline.description: An employee receives a call from someone claiming to be from the IT helpdesk about a security issue on their machine.

timeline.title: Urgency Created
timeline.description: The caller insists the employee's machine is actively compromised and action must be taken immediately.

timeline.title: Remote Access Requested
timeline.description: The caller asks the employee to download and install a remote desktop tool to 'fix the issue'.

timeline.title: Credential Request
timeline.description: Once remote access is established, the caller requests the employee read out their password to 'verify identity'.

timeline.title: Suspicious Activity Flagged
timeline.description: A colleague overhears the call and alerts the real IT team who confirm no call was made from their department.

timeline.title: Incident Contained
timeline.description: Remote session is terminated, password is reset, and the incident is formally reported.

node-1.title: Unexpected IT Support Call
node-1.situation: An employee receives an unsolicited phone call from someone identifying themselves as a member of the IT helpdesk. The caller says they have detected 'abnormal activity' on the employee's workstation and must resolve it urgently. The caller ID shows an internal number.
node-1.question: What should the employee do?

options.label: A
options.text: Politely end the call and phone the IT helpdesk back using the number listed in the internal directory to verify the call is legitimate.

options.label: B
options.text: Cooperate with the caller — caller ID shows an internal number so it must be legitimate.

options.label: C
options.text: Give the caller a different employee's credentials to test them first.

node-2.title: IT Confirms No Outgoing Call
node-2.situation: IT confirms no such call was made from their team. The caller spoofed an internal extension number. Another employee in the same office did follow the instructions and has installed an unknown remote access tool.
node-2.question: What is the most urgent action for the affected workstation?

options.label: A
options.text: Immediately disconnect the workstation from the network, terminate any active remote sessions, and escalate to the incident response team.

options.label: B
options.text: Uninstall the remote access tool and restart the machine — that should be sufficient.

node-3.title: Extent of Access Assessed
node-3.situation: Forensics shows the attacker had remote control of the machine for 8 minutes before disconnection. Files were browsed and clipboard data may have been captured.
node-3.question: What further containment steps are required?

options.label: A
options.text: Reset all credentials the affected employee may have typed or stored on that machine, review logs for data exfiltration, and reimage the workstation.

options.label: B
options.text: Only reset the employee's main account password — the session was short.

node-4.title: Caller ID Spoofing Awareness
node-4.situation: Staff are surprised that an internal number could be spoofed. Many employees assume calls from internal extensions are always trustworthy.
node-4.question: What policy change should address this misconception?

options.label: A
options.text: Publish a clear policy stating IT will never ask for passwords over the phone, and train staff that caller ID can be spoofed.

options.label: B
options.text: Install call-blocking software to prevent all unknown numbers from reaching staff.

node-5.title: Scenario Complete
node-5.situation: The compromised workstation has been reimaged, credentials reset, and logs reviewed. A new vishing awareness policy is in place, and staff now know that IT will never request passwords or remote access during unsolicited calls.
node-5.question: Scenario complete. Well done!
